segments.s752-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در خانه شد is a classical idiom meaning 'became in the house' → 'entered/went into the house' with شد as the past verb. آتشی بر فروخت is a clause meaning 'a fire flared up / was kindled there': آتشی ('a burning/fire'), بر as a prepositional/particle 'upon/up', and فروخت (here from فروزیدن) meaning 'kindled / flared up'.
segments.s752-g90: همه آلت خویش is the subject phrase 'all his members/limbs' with the possessive clitic خویش (displayed as خوی + ش). یکسر is an adverb 'completely/utterly' modifying the verb; بسوخت is the past-tense intransitive 'was burned / burned up' (3rd person), so the clause means 'all his members were entirely burned.'
segments.s752-g41: 'کجا' is an interrogative/exclamatory adverb 'where' introducing a rhetorical question; the verb (copula) is omitted by poetic ellipsis. 'اسپ شبدیز' is a noun phrase with a proper name (Shabdiz) following the common noun (steed). 'و زرین رکیب' joins with 'و' and places the adjective زرین before the noun رکیب (golden rider/companion).
segments.s752-g42: 'که' begins a relative clause modifying the preceding noun phrase ('who/which'). 'زیر تو' = 'under you' (subject to your command), where 'زیر' has the sense 'subject to / under the control of'. 'اندر' = 'in/among', introducing the circumstance; 'بدی' = 'adversity/evil' (object of the state); 'ناشکیب' = 'impatient, restless' describes the subject. The clause has a compressed, poetic order: adjective after noun phrase (بدی ناشکیب = 'impatient in adversity').
segments.s752-g43: 'کجا' again sets up the rhetorical question. 'آن سواران' = 'those horsemen/riders'; 'زرین' is the adjective 'golden' modifying سواران; 'ستام' is a past-participial adjective (from ستاییدن) meaning 'praised' or 'renowned' and follows the noun as a descriptive participle.
segments.s752-g44: 'که' introduces a relative clause. The phrase is compressed and somewhat idiomatic: 'دشمن بدی' = 'evil/enemy' (here, 'the hostile enemy'); 'تیغشان را' = 'their sword' with the object marker را; 'نیام' is the noun 'scabbard/sheath'. Poetic ellipsis leaves out a verb; the intended sense is that the enemies could not put the swords into their scabbards (i.e., the swords remained unsheathed, emphasizing constant combat).
segments.s752-g45: 'کجا آن همه راز و آن بخردی' repeats the formulaic 'where is all that...' 'راز' = 'secret/secret knowledge'; 'بخردی' = 'wisdom/prudence' (a noun formed with the prefix ب- here functioning as part of the abstract noun). The two noun phrases are linked by 'و' and the demonstrative 'آن' points back to the formerly described qualities.
segments.s752-g46: 'کجا آن همه فره ایزدی' again asks 'where is all that...' 'فره' = 'farr' meaning 'divine glory/splendor'; 'ایزدی' is an adjective 'divine' or 'of the deity' modifying فره. The word order (noun then adjective) is a common poetic ordering for set phrases (فره‌ایزدی = 'divine farr').
segments.s752-g47: 'کجا آن همه بخشش روز بزم' follows the same rhetorical pattern. 'بخشش' here means 'generosity/gifts' (donations or largesse given at a feast); 'روز بزم' = 'the day of the banquet/assembly' (روز = day; بزم = feast/banquet) — together indicating generosity shown at gatherings. Word order places the time-specifying phrase after the noun in poetic style.
segments.s752-g48: 'کجا آن همه کوشش روز رزم' mirrors the previous line: 'کوشش' = 'effort/striving'; 'روز رزم' = 'the day of battle' (a genitive/time phrase). The phrase asks rhetorically where all that martial endeavor has gone. The construction is parallel and elliptic, typical of Ferdowsi's lamenting catalogue.
segments.s752-g33: کجا is the interrogative 'where' starting a rhetorical question; آن is the demonstrative 'that/those' pointing to previously mentioned people; غلامان is the plural noun 'servants/pages/men‑at‑court' (here 'pages/soldiers'); زرین کمر is a compound modifier meaning 'golden‑belted' (زرین 'golden' modifying کمر 'belt') placed after the noun in a poetic/compound style. The whole phrase is the noun phrase questioned by کجا.
segments.s752-g34: کجا again opens a parallel question; آن همه is a quantifying demonstrative 'all those' (همه 'all'); رای و آیین و فر is a coordinated list: رای here means 'plan/counsel', آیین 'ritual/custom', and فر (here written وِفر) is the archaic poetic noun 'divine glory/splendour'. The conjunction و links the nouns; the whole list is being lamented as missing.
segments.s752-g35: کجا 'where' begins the question; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; سرافراز is an adjective 'proud/exalted/honored' modifying the following noun; جانوسپار (classical spelling of جان‌سپار) is a compound noun meaning 'one who gives his life/one devoted to sacrifice' — together they name the exalted, self‑devoting figure being asked about.
segments.s752-g36: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause describing the person; با ... بود is a simple past possession/state construction 'was with' meaning 'had' or 'possessed': با تخت زر بود = 'he had (a) golden throne'. The repeated با before گوشوار parallels the first with and links the second possessed item; بود is past 'was', and گوشوار means 'ornaments' (literally 'earring' but used broadly here).
segments.s752-g37: کجا 'where' + آن همه 'all those' again opens a lamenting question; لشکر 'army/host' is first in a coordinted list linked by و; بوم و بر is a fixed poetic pair meaning 'homeland and domain/territory' (broadly 'lands'). The nouns are listed without an explicit verb, as in many Persian rhetorical questions.
segments.s752-g38: کجا again begins the parallel clause; آن سرافرازی is the abstract noun 'that pride/honour' (سرافرازی formed from سرافراز), and تخت زر repeats the image 'golden throne' (تخت 'throne' + زر 'gold') — a terse coordinate phrase asking where those symbols of rule and glory have gone.
segments.s752-g39: کجا 'where' + آن 'that' begins the question; سر خود literally 'the head itself' (سر 'head' + خود 'own/itself') here stands for the person's person or life; و links to زرین زره, 'golden armor' (زرین 'golden' modifying زره 'armor'). The structure lists physical possessions/person of the vanished hero.
segments.s752-g40: ز is the poetic form of از 'from/of' indicating material or composition; گوهر 'gems/jewels' names what the armor is set with; فگنده is an archaic/perfect participle 'thrown/laid' (i.e., 'set/strewn'); گره بر گره is an idiom 'knot upon knot' (reduplicative structure with بر 'on/upon' between repeated گره), describing the intricately linked or studded pattern. Together the phrase describes armor encrusted with gems in layered knots.
segments.s752-g65: هر آنکس که … شنود is a relative clause starting with هر آنکس که = 'whoever/anyone who'. او here is a resumptive/demonstrative third-person pronoun referring back to the person/figure in question and precedes the possessed noun کارِ خسرو ('the affair/deeds of Khosrow'); classical Persian often places the possessor phrase before the verb. شنود is an archaic/poetic third-person singular present (or gnomic) form of شنیدن 'to hear' and completes the clause 'whoever hears (of) Khosrow's deeds'.
segments.s752-g66: به گیتی places the attitude/location 'in/with respect to the world'; نبایدش is the classical impersonal prohibition ('it ought not be for him' → 'he should not'), the enclitic -ش refers back to the person of the relative clause. گستاخ is the predicate adjective 'insolent/impudent', and بود is the copula; together نبایدش گستاخ بود = 'he ought not to be arrogant'.
segments.s752-g67: همه بوم ایران is a quantified genitive phrase 'the whole land of Iran'. The sequence تو ویران شمر is vocative/appositive: تو 'you' is the addressed subject, ویران is a predicative adjective 'ruined' (the verb 'to make/leave ruinous' is implied), and شمر is the name/appellation placed after the predicate for emphasis: 'You, Shamr, (left) Iran ruined.'
segments.s752-g68: کنام پلنگان و شیران شمر is a locative-predicative phrase: کنام 'the bosom/den/haunt (of)', followed by the genitive/associates 'پلنگان و شیران' (leopards and lions), with شمر as the final noun that serves as the subject/predicate nominal — i.e. 'Shamr is (in) the lair of leopards and lions' (copula omitted). This inversion (place + animals + name) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s752-g69: سر تخم ساسانیان بود شاه is a nominal clause with بود as copula: سر here means 'chief/leader', تخم ساسانیان 'the stock/seed of the Sasanians' (genitive), and بود شاه = 'was king'. Word order is head/possessive + copula + predicate noun: 'The leader of the Sasanian line was a king.'
segments.s752-g70: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. چون here functions temporally ('when' / 'after'), او 'he' refers back to that king, and نبیند is the classical negative present/gnomic form of دیدن used in narrative sense ('he was not seen' / 'he did not appear again'). دگر تاج و گاه = 'another crown and throne' is the object/phrase governed by the negative: idiomatically 'when he was no longer seen there was no other crown or seat.'
segments.s752-g71: شد is the past/become verb 'became'; این تخمه is the subject 'this lineage/offspring', and ویران is the predicative adjective 'ruinous/ruined'. The clause continues with و ایران همان — the coordinating و and همان ('the same/likewise') indicate that Iran suffered the same fate. The clause therefore reads 'This stock became ruined, and Iran the same.'
segments.s752-g72: برآمد is an intransitive verb meaning 'turned out/arose/was realized'; همه کامه is the subject phrase 'all (their) desires/hopes' (کامه here = 'desires' or 'hopes'), and بدگمان is the predicate adjective 'doubtful/suspicious'. The literal sense is 'all hopes turned out doubtful.'
segments.s752-g17: همی‌گفت is an archaic/perfective-imperfective marker meaning 'kept saying' (habitual past); it precedes the quoted exclamation. الایا is an interjection ('O! / behold!') used to call attention. ردا is a noun 'robe/cloak' placed after the interjection as an object/exclamation. The sequence is a string of vocative or exclamatory items introduced by the reporting verb.
segments.s752-g18: This is a run of nominal descriptors in apposition or as further exclamations: بزرگا (pl./collective 'great ones'), سترگا (archaic plural for 'eyes'), تناور ('tall, towering') modify or enumerate the mourned objects; گوا here is a short particle meaning 'it seems / apparently' that closes the exclamation. Word order is a poetic piling up of nouns/adjectives without an explicit copula.
segments.s752-g19: کجات is a fused/poetic interrogative 'where?' placed at the start of the clause. آن ('that/the') is a demonstrative determiner modifying بزرگی ('greatness/ grandeur'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. آن again marks دستگاه ('the court/establishment') — device/court is the head noun. The clause asks the location/existence of these two concrete abstractions.
segments.s752-g20: Another interrogative with parallel structure: کجات 'where' + آن 'that' + همه 'all/whole' introduces a list of items (فر 'royal glory', تخت 'throne', کلاه 'headgear/crown'). The repeated کجات at the clause start is a rhetorical device (anaphora) emphasizing the questioning tone.
segments.s752-g21: کجات (where) introduces a list with آن همه ('that all/those') followed by nouns in sequence: برز ('high/exalted'), بالا ('lofty/up'), and تاج ('crown'). The nouns are coordinated by و and form a single nominal phrase questioned by the interrogative at the start.
segments.s752-g22: Parallel clause beginning with کجات; آن همه introduces یاره ('attendants/followers') and then تخت عاج ('throne of ivory') joined by و. عاج (ivory) is a postmodifier of تخت, forming a compound noun 'ivory throne.'
segments.s752-g23: کجات 'where' + آن همه 'all that' introduces an enumerated nominal phrase: مردی ('manliness / valour'), زور ('strength / might'), و فر ('and royal glory'). The coordinated nouns list virtues/attributes whose absence is being lamented.
segments.s752-g24: جهان را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the world' as the object; همی‌داشتی is the imperfective/past habitual 'you used to hold/possess.' زیر پر is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'under the wing' (زیر = under; پر = feather/wing) describing how the world was held — this closes the relative/circumstantial idea attached to the preceding nouns.
segments.s752-g73: فزون زین (literally ‘more than this’) is a comparative phrase placed before the verb; نباشد is a negative jussive/present-subjunctive meaning ‘there shall not be’ or ‘should not be’. کسی را uses را as an object/emphasis marker attached to کسی (‘anyone’), and سپاه is the noun ‘army’ that is predicated here — the whole string means ‘There should be no army for anyone greater than this.’
segments.s752-g74: ز لشکر = ‘from the ranks/host’ (poetic use of ز = از). که introduces a relative/clause of explanation ‘such that/that’, آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش ‘came to him/it’, and فریادخواه is a substantive meaning ‘one who cries for help / a petitioner’. The clause explains the undesirable consequence: ‘that a petitioner came to him from the host.’
segments.s752-g75: گزند آمد is a simple verbal clause ‘harm came’ (گزند = ‘harm’ as subject, آمد = ‘came’/‘befell’). از پاسبان بزرگ = ‘from the great guard/guardian’ (از = from, پاسبان = guard/protector, بزرگ = great). The line states the source of the harm.
segments.s752-g76: کنون = ‘now/presently’; اندر آید = ‘comes in/enters’ (اندر = in/into, poetic preposition, آید = present tense ‘comes’); سوی رخنه گرگ = ‘toward/into the breach the wolf’ (سوی = toward, رخنه = breach, گرگ = wolf). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: the verb follows اندر and the agent (گرگ) comes at the end.
segments.s752-g77: نباشد here functions as a negative present/subjunctive meaning ‘will not be’/‘shall not remain’; سپاه تو = ‘your army’ (سپاه = army, تو = your); هم = ‘also/even’; پایدار = ‘stable/lasting/steadfast’. Together: ‘Your army too will not be steadfast.’
segments.s752-g78: چو = ‘when’ (temporal), برخیزد is subjunctive/present ‘arises/comes up’ (برخیزد = to rise/occur), از چار سو = ‘from four sides’ (چار = four, سو = side/direction), کارزار = ‘battle/field of combat’. The clause gives the condition: ‘when battle arises from the four sides.’
segments.s752-g79: روان تو را = ‘your soul’ with را marking the direct object (روان = soul/spirit, تو = your). دادگر یار = ‘a just friend/ally’ (دادگر = just, یار = companion/ally). باد is the optative particle ‘may (it be)’. The structure is an optative blessing: ‘May a just companion be for your soul.’
segments.s752-g80: سر بد سگالان = ‘the bad/ill heads of the Sagalan’ (سر = head, بد = bad/evil, سگالان = proper name/plural); نگونسار = adjective ‘woeful/ill‑fated’ modifying those heads; باد again is the optative ‘may (it be)’. The clause is a curse or wish of misfortune toward the named enemies.
segments.s752-g49: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. آن همه is a demonstrative + quantifier 'all those'. راهوار is an adjectival/poetic qualifier meaning 'fit for the road, road-worthy' placed before the noun استران (steeds); Persian permits the adjective before the noun here. The whole phrase asks after the location/existence of those horses.
segments.s752-g50: This is a compressed noun phrase describing things associated with the horses: عماری زرین is literally 'golden buildings/structures' (poetically 'golden stalls' or 'gold-work fittings') and فرمانبران is an adjective/noun meaning 'obedient, command-following'. The و links the two descriptors; both follow the previous noun phrase to continue the lament.
segments.s752-g51: This is an enumerative noun phrase: هیونان 'beasts/animals' joined by و to other items (بالا and وپیل). سپید is a postpositive adjective modifying the immediately preceding noun (پیل/elephant) so 'the white elephant'. The nouns are listed without verbs, typical in poetic catalogues.
segments.s752-g52: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. گشته is the perfect participle/auxiliary meaning 'have become' or 'are turned (into)'; together they yield 'all have become...'. از جان تو ناامید is a prepositional complement: از introduces the domain ('about/from'), جان تو 'your life/being' is the object of that preposition, and ناامید is the predicate adjective 'hopeless' — 'have all become hopeless concerning you' (i.e. given up on you).
segments.s752-g53: Another interrogative 'کجا آن' ('where are those'). سخنها is plural 'the speeches/words'. به + شیرین زبان is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with a sweet tongue' or 'sweet‑spoken' (here به functions like 'with/by' to mark manner). شیرین modifies زبان, producing the compound idea 'sweet‑tongued'.
segments.s752-g54: The repeated 'کجا آن' introduces a parallel list. دل و رای و روشن‌روان are three coordinated nouns: دل 'heart', رای 'counsel/opinion' and روشن‌روان a compound meaning 'bright‑soul' or 'cheerful spirit'. The coordination emphasizes the loss of inner qualities as well as speech.
segments.s752-g55: ز (poetic form of از) marks separation/source: 'from everything' (ز هر چیز). تنها 'alone' modifies the subject. چرا is the interrogative 'why' and ماندی is the 2nd‑person past 'you remained/you were left' — the word order (preposing ز هر چیز تنها چرا) is emphatic/poetic, placing the circumstance before the verb.
segments.s752-g56: ز دفتر is 'from the book' or 'in the register/record' (ز = 'from/in' here). چنین روز is the object 'such a day'. کی is the interrogative 'when' and خواندی is 2nd‑person past 'did you read/recite' (here 'read' or 'find on record'); together they form the question 'when did you ever read such a day in the book?' — a rhetorical challenge.
segments.s752-g9: زمانی is a temporal adverb 'for a while' modifying the verb. همی‌بود is a literary imperfective/past construction ('was/kept being'); the preverbal همی marks continued/progressive aspect. در پیش شاه is a locative phrase ('in the presence of the king') with در + پیش meaning 'before/in the presence of'. Word order here places the time and state before the locative phrase.
segments.s752-g10: خروشان is a participial/manner phrase 'roaring' (used adverbially) that modifies the verb بیامد. بیامد is simple past 'came'. سوی بارگاه is a prepositional goal 'toward the palace/royal court'. The segment expresses manner + motion toward a destination.
segments.s752-g11: همی is a continuative adverb 'still/continually'. پهلوانی here is a verbal noun 'heroism/heroic behaviour' functioning as the sphere/character of the action. برو appears as an archaic element forming a verbal expression with پهلوانی (roughly 'to act heroically' or 'in heroism'); مویه کرد is the main verbal phrase 'made lamentation/wailed' (مویه = lament as noun, کرد = 'did/made' used as light verb). The overall construction is: temporal adverb + domain ('heroism') + compound + light-verb 'made lament'.
segments.s752-g12: دو رخساره is a numeral + noun 'two cheeks/faces' (numeral before the noun). زرد is a predicate/adjective 'pale' describing رخساره. و links the next noun phrase. دلی پر ز درد is a standard pattern: دلی (a form of 'heart') + پر 'full' + ز 'of' + درد 'pain' = 'a heart full of pain'; note پر ز is a poetic way to say 'full of'.
segments.s752-g13: چنان + بد + که is a result clause: 'so/such ... that'. بد is the adjective 'bad/so' here forming the intensifier with چنان. زاریش is زاری + enclitic -ش 'his lament/wailing' functioning as the object. بشنید is simple past 'he heard'. شاه (the subject) follows the verb in this classical word order.
segments.s752-g14: همان کس 'that same person' is the topical subject. کجا is the interrogative 'where' placed before the verb داشت. داشت ... نگاه is the verb complex 'had/kept (a) gaze' (داشت plus the noun نگاه), with او را the definite object 'him' marked by را. The line reads as a rhetorical question about where that person directed his gaze.
segments.s752-g15: نگهبان 'the guards' is the subject. که here functions as a conjunction meaning 'when/while'. بودند is past plural 'they were / were present'. گریان شدند is a compound verb 'became weeping' (گریان = weeping; شدند = became) indicating a change of state: they started to weep.
segments.s752-g16: چو is a conjunction 'when/as'. بر آتش مهر is a metaphorical locative/ablative phrase 'on the fire of love' (بر + آتش + مهر). بریان شد(ند) is a stative/resultative: بریان 'roasted/scorched' + شدند 'became' = 'were/ became roasted' — a poetic way to say they were consumed/scorched by love.
segments.s752-g1: کنون is a temporal adverb placed clause-initially ('now'). شیون باربد is a genitive-like phrase ('the wailing of Barbad') with the possessor Barbad placed after the noun (classical/poetic word order, ezāfe is omitted). گوش دار is an imperative idiom ('keep an ear' → 'listen'), where دار functions as an imperative of داشتن ('hold, keep') addressed to the addressee.
segments.s752-g2: سر مهتران را is the definite object (را marks the object) meaning 'the heads of the mahterān' (possessor follows noun; مهتران = 'chiefs/elders'—an epic/polite social group). به آغوش = 'into/to the embrace' (به = toward/into + آغوش = embrace). دار again is the imperative 'hold/keep' (imperative second person), so the whole segment commands to put/keep the chiefs' heads in the embrace.
segments.s752-g3: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' at clause start. آگاه شد is a past-tense verbal phrase ('became aware/was informed'); the subject باربد follows the verb (postposed subject, common in epic Persian). زانک here is a subordinating conjunction ('that / because') introducing the content linked to شاه (the king) that follows in the next clause.
segments.s752-g4: بپرداخت is a past tense 3rd-person form of پرداختن with the verbal prefix بـ; here it means 'set about, gave vent to or addressed.' بیداد و بی‌کام گاه is the compound object/complement: بیداد used poetically as an 'outcry/woe' and بی‌کام گاه = 'an occasion/time of unfulfillment' (بی‌کام modifies گاه). The coordination (و) links the two nouns as the things he 'poured forth' or 'expressed'.
segments.s752-g5: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' بیامد is simple past 'he came' (3rd sg.). سوی طیسفون is a following directional prepositional phrase 'toward Ctesiphon.' The subject is omitted (understood from previous lines); the verb-first order (prepositional phrase + verb + direction) is a normal poetic sequence.
segments.s752-g6: پر از ... is the adjectival predicate 'full of ...' (پر 'full' + از 'of'). آب مژگان is a poetic genitive 'the water of the eyelashes' = 'tears' (ezāfe omitted). The clause then parallels with و دل پر ز خون where ز (= از) introduces the source 'of blood': 'and a heart full from/of blood.' Parallel structure and slight variation (از vs ز) are common in classical style.
segments.s752-g7: بیامد بدان خانه is 'he came to that house' — بدان is a contracted classical form (به + آن = 'to that'). او را بدید is the following clause 'he saw him/her' with را as the definite object marker; the sequence Verb — to-place — object-verb is normal narrative ordering in Ferdowsi.
segments.s752-g8: شده is the perfect/prime verbal form 'had become' or 'became.' لعل رخسار او is a poetic noun phrase 'his cheek (رخسار) a ruby (لعل)' with possessive pronoun after the noun (classical order). شنبلید is a (archaic/poetic) noun (a plant—fenugreek/its ear) placed after the clause in apposition/simile; this line uses an image (cheek = ruby; compared to the ear/spike of a plant) and contains archaic vocabulary.
segments.s752-g57: مبادا که introduces a prohibitive clause meaning 'lest' / 'do not'; گستاخ is an adjective 'insolent/impudent'; باشی is the present-subjunctive/hortative 'you be' used after مبادا. به + دهر places the attitude toward the world/age ('toward the world' or 'in the world').
segments.s752-g58: که here gives the reason ('for/because'); زهرش is 'its poison' (figurative malice); فزون آمد is a past/simple phrase 'increased/grew'; از پای زهر means 'beyond the limit/extent of poison' (از + پای = 'beyond the foot/limit of'), a poetic way to say its poison has grown past measure.
segments.s752-g59: پسر خواستی is past 'you wanted (a) son'; تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that') and is followed by بود 'would be/was' describing the hoped-for role; یار و پشت are two nouns linked by و: 'a companion and a support' (پشت = support/protection).
segments.s752-g60: کنون 'now' sets the time; از پسر 'from the son' shows the source; رنجت is 'your suffering/trouble'; آمد is simple past 'came' and به مشت is an idiomatic phrase 'in the form of a fist / as a blow' — i.e. the son's behaviour has become a blow/trouble to you.
segments.s752-g61: ز فرزند 'from offspring/children' gives the source; شاهان 'kings' is the subject; به نیرو شوند is a verbal phrase 'become to/into strength' — i.e. 'gain strength' or 'grow powerful' (شوند is the plural present/subjunctive/predicative 'become').
segments.s752-g62: ز رنج زمانه 'from the suffering/hardships of the times' gives the cause; بی is the negative prefix 'without'; آهو (here figurative) means 'gazelle' used as 'beloved/beauty/grace' and شوند 'they become' — so the clause says they are left 'without the gazelle' (bereft of beloved/grace) because of time's hardships.
segments.s752-g63: شهنشاه را uses را to topicalize/emphasize 'the emperor'; چونک functions as 'since/when'; نیرو بکاست is a past construction 'his strength decreased' (بکاست = decreased), so the clause means 'when/since the king's strength lessened.'
segments.s752-g64: چو 'when'; بالای فرزند او 'the elevation/position of his son' (بالای = high place/status of the son, او = his); گشت راست is a verbal phrase 'became/right' i.e. 'became firm/established' — together: 'when his son's position became secure/raised.'
segments.s752-g81: به here is the preposition 'by' introducing an oath/invocation ('by God…'). The phrase تو‌ای شهریار is a vocative: تو (you) + ای (vocative particle) + شهریار (king), addressing the ruler directly. Word order places the invoked authorities/objects first ('به یزدان و نام تو') for emphasis.
segments.s752-g82: This is a continuation of the same enumerative invocation: repeated به (= 'by') introduces each item (Nowruz, مهر, then by the joyful spring). The coordinating و links the items; خرم qualifies بهار ('joyful spring'). The repetition of به for rhythm and solemnity is a poetic device.
segments.s752-g83: که‌گر is the fused conditional particle (که + گر) meaning 'if'. دستِ من ('my hand') functions as subject meaning 'my power/ability' here. زین سپس نیز رود = 'from this point onward also goes/withdraws': زین is an archaic/poetic 'from this' (literally 'of this'), سپس 'after/then', نیز 'also', and رود is the verb 'go/withdraw' (here a potential/subjunctive idea — 'should go').
segments.s752-g84: بساید is a jussive/optative verbal form meaning 'avail/benefit' ('may it avail'). مبادا is a negative jussive particle 'may it not be / heaven forbid'. The phrase به من بر درود literally 'fall upon me' — به من 'to me', بر 'upon', درود here 'blessing/greeting' — so the whole clause is a negative wish: 'may it not be of benefit to me, may no blessing fall upon me.' Note the word-order inversion (بساید before مبادا) for poetic emphasis.
segments.s752-g85: بسوزم is an optative/jussive verb form 'may I burn' / 'I will burn' used here emphatically. همه آلت خویش را is the direct object ('all my means/implements/possessions'): همه 'all', آلت 'means/instrument(s)', خویش + ش (split here) = 'one's own', and را is the definite object marker. The object follows the verb, as is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s752-g86: بدان here means 'by that / thereby' (literally 'with that') introducing purpose/result; تا introduces a purposive clause 'so that / in order that'. نبینم is the negative subjunctive 'I do not see' (literally 'may I not see' or 'I will not see'), and بداندیش را is the object 'the ill‑wisher' with را marking it. Together: 'for that, so that I do not see the ill‑wisher.'
segments.s752-g87: ببرید is a literary past (3rd person) form meaning 'cut off' (here used as a narrative past); the verb precedes its object, a common order in classical Persian. هر چارانگشت خویش is a noun phrase 'each/every four-finger(s) of his own' with خویش ('his own') attached as a possessive; the phrase is the verb's object.
segments.s752-g88: بریده is the past-participle used as a noun 'the cut-off / severed (pieces)'; همی‌داشت is a past-progressive construction (همی- prefix + داشت) meaning 'was holding' or 'kept'. در مشت خویش is a prepositional phrase 'in his fist', where مشت is 'fist' and خویش (split into خوی + ش) supplies the possessive 'his'.
segments.s752-g25: کجا is an interrogative adverb 'where'. آن is a demonstrative ('that/those') used emphatically before the noun phrase. There is no copular verb — this is an elliptical, rhetorical question common in classical Persian: the speaker lists nouns (شبستان 'inner chamber/banqueting hall' and رامشگران 'comforters/peacemakers') after the question word to mean 'where are those ...?'. The conjunction و simply links the two coordinated nouns.
segments.s752-g26: Again کجا introduces a parallel elliptical question; آن marks the demonstrative applying to the whole noun cluster. بر here is used poetically as a nominal element ('the heights/top') and is coordinated with بارگاه ('palace, royal court') by و; سران ('leaders/chiefs') is in apposition or governs the phrase as the possessive/relational target ('the palaces of the chiefs'). The phrase therefore reads as a coordinated noun phrase 'the heights and the palaces of the chiefs', with verbless interrogation.
segments.s752-g27: کجا begins the question. افسر here is a head-ornament/helmet (a common epic usage) and is coordinated with کاویانی درفش: کاویانی modifies درفش ('the Kayanian/Kavian banner/standard'). There is no overt verb; the two coordinated items are simply listed as the objects of the rhetorical 'where'.
segments.s752-g28: کجا again opens the second parallel question. آن همه is an emphatic demonstrative + quantifier ('all those' / 'so many'). تیغهای is the plural of تیغ ('swords, blades'); بنفش is an adjective ('purple') placed after the noun, as is normal in poetic Persian, giving 'purple swords'. The clause is verbless and elliptical: 'Where are all those purple swords?'
segments.s752-g29: کجا opens the question; آن marks the demonstrative. دلیران is the plural adjective/noun 'the brave ones'. جنگ آوران is a compound-like sequence (جنگ + آوران) functioning as an agentive: آوران ('bringers/carriers') attaches to جنگ to mean 'war-bringers' i.e. 'warriors' or 'fighters'. The two nouns form a coordinated description: 'those brave war-fighters'.
segments.s752-g30: کجا plus آن begins the parallel clause. The sequence رد و موبد و مهتران lists three coordinated items joined by و. موبد is a technical term meaning a Zoroastrian priest; مهتران is a plural meaning 'the great/elders/chiefs' (an honorific plural). رد is problematic in this token set (none of the provided senses clearly matches the funeral/courtly meaning 'robe' or 'vestment'); here it behaves as a noun in a list of courtly/ceremonial items or persons. The entire clause is a verbless list posed as a rhetorical 'where are ...?'.
segments.s752-g31: کجا آن همه again introduces an emphatic plural list. بزم ('banquet, feast') is a common term for courtly assemblies; وساز here stands for musical instruments or musicians (in epic diction the word groups with بزم), and شکار is 'hunt' (either the act of hunting or the game). The three nouns are coordinated to enumerate courtly pastimes and martial leisure. The clause is elliptical: 'Where are all those feasts, instruments, and hunts?'
segments.s752-g32: کجا آن opens the final parallel clause. خرامیدن is an infinitive/verbal noun meaning 'striding, strutting' and is used substantively here; کارزار ('battlefield, battle') is the domain or scene associated with that motion. The phrase thus reads as a nominal compound — 'the strutting of the battle' — again posed without an explicit verb as a rhetorical question.

sentences.s752-s45: When he entered the house a fire flared up there, and all his members were utterly burned.
sentences.s752-s21: Where is the steed Shabdiz and the golden rider who, under you, were impatient in adversity?
sentences.s752-s22: Where are those golden, praised horsemen whose enemies could not sheathe their swords?
sentences.s752-s23: Where is all that secrecy and that wisdom? Where is all that divine glory?
sentences.s752-s24: Where is all that generosity of the feast-day? Where is all that striving on the day of battle?
sentences.s752-s17: Where are those golden‑belted servants? Where is all that counsel, ceremony, and divine glory?
sentences.s752-s18: Where is that proud, life‑devoting man who had a golden throne and ornaments?
sentences.s752-s19: Where are all those armies and homelands and domains? Where is that pride and golden throne?
sentences.s752-s20: Where is that head itself and the golden armor, set with gems knot upon knot?
sentences.s752-s33: Anyone who hears of Khosrow's deeds should not be arrogant in the world.
sentences.s752-s34: You, Shamr, laid waste the whole land of Iran; Shamr became the lair of leopards and lions.
sentences.s752-s35: The head of the Sasanian stock was a king; when he was no longer seen there was no other crown or throne.
sentences.s752-s36: This stock became ruined, and Iran likewise; all hopes (desires) turned out doubtful.
sentences.s752-s9: He kept crying aloud, “Alaya! the robe — the great kings, the bright‑eyed, the towering, it seems!”
sentences.s752-s10: Where is that greatness and that court? Where are all that glory and throne and crown?
sentences.s752-s11: Where are all that height and loftiness and crown? Where are all those followers and the ivory throne?
sentences.s752-s12: Where is all that manliness and might and royal glory by which you used to hold the world beneath your wing?
sentences.s752-s37: Let no one's army be greater than this, so that a petitioner should come to it from the ranks.
sentences.s752-s38: Harm came from the great watchman; now the wolf approaches the breach.
sentences.s752-s39: Your army will not remain steadfast either when the battle arises from all four sides.
sentences.s752-s40: May a just companion be with your soul; may the heads of the ill‑fated Sagalan be woeful.
sentences.s752-s25: Where are all those road-worthy steeds, their golden stalls and obedient (followers)?
sentences.s752-s26: Beasts, (the) high (ones) and the white elephant — all have become hopeless about you.
sentences.s752-s27: Where are those speeches in sweet tongue? Where are those heart, counsel, and bright‑souls?
sentences.s752-s28: Why have you remained alone, separated from everything? When did you ever read such a day in the book?
sentences.s752-s5: For a while he remained in the king's presence; then, roaring, he came toward the royal court.
sentences.s752-s6: He continued in his heroism and then lamented; two pallid cheeks and a heart full of pain.
sentences.s752-s7: It was such (a thing) that the king heard his lament; that very man — where did he fix his gaze upon him?
sentences.s752-s8: The guards, when they were there, began to weep; as if they were roasted on the fire of love.
sentences.s752-s1: Now listen to Barbad's lament; hold the chiefs' heads to your bosom.
sentences.s752-s2: When Barbad learned this, because the king had ... he uttered cries of grief and a time of disappointment.
sentences.s752-s3: He came from Jahrom toward Ctesiphon, eyes full of water from his lashes and a heart full of blood.
sentences.s752-s4: He came to that house and saw him; his cheek had become ruby-like, a spike of fenugreek.
sentences.s752-s29: Do not be insolent toward the world, for its poison has increased beyond measure.
sentences.s752-s30: You wanted a son so that he would be a friend and support; now from that son your trouble has come as a blow.
sentences.s752-s31: From offspring kings gain strength; from the hardships of the age they become deprived of their gazelle (their beloved/grace).
sentences.s752-s32: When the emperor's strength declined, and when his son's rank was set upright.
sentences.s752-s41: By God and by your name, O king; by Nowruz and by affection, and by the joyful spring.
sentences.s752-s42: If my hand (my power) from this point forth should also go away, may it not profit me — may no blessing fall upon me.
sentences.s752-s43: I will burn all my means (all my possessions) for that, so that I do not see the ill‑wisher.
sentences.s752-s44: He cut off each of his four fingers and kept the severed pieces in his fist.
sentences.s752-s13: Where are those inner halls and the comforters, where those heights and the palaces of the chiefs?
sentences.s752-s14: Where is the helmet and the Kayanian standard, where are all those purple swords?
sentences.s752-s15: Where are those brave warriors of battle, where are that robe (?), the mobeds and the chiefs?
sentences.s752-s16: Where are all those banquets, instruments, and hunts; where is the proud striding of the battlefield?
